data.title: HIVE Digital: Great Seasonality And A Bullish Chart Setup
data.description:
Back in January, I covered small-cap Bitcoin miner HIVE Digital (HIVE), calling it a strong buy at the time. I had my reasons, including firm price support at $2.80. That didn’t work out, however, as the massive slide in equities globally into the April low saw HIVE blow right through $2.80 on the way to $1.26. Events like that are why we use stops, and why we don’t become bag holders. At any rate, this stock is well-off from where I called it a strong buy; has my view changed? Not really, and below I’ll detail why the value proposition is even better today than it was five months ago.

An Extremely Bullish Setup

I recognize my last call was wrong, and that’s fine; it happens. I noted the line in the sand was $2.80, and if you sold at that point, your losses were minimized. Today, my line in the sand is the rising 50-day SMA, which we can see below in blue.

That level is currently $1.77, which is 12 cents below where the stock closed on Friday. It’s also rising, so it will gradually drift higher as the days go on. I would not hold HIVE under that line.

Now, why do I say this is extremely bullish? We have a series of higher lows, two rising moving averages, and the 20-day EMA ahead of the 50-day SMA. So long as the 50-day SMA holds on any pullbacks, this is a textbook uptrend setup.

The momentum indicators are mixed. The PPO is bullish, the RSI is neutral, and the Chaikin Money Flow index is bearish. With that in mind, I’d become more bullish if we had a sustained move in the RSI ahead of 60, and a reading above zero on the CMF. But for now, the price chart itself is enough for me, and I really like this chart.

Bullish Seasonality And A Stock That’s Just Too Cheap

Before we look at the fundamentals, I’d be remiss if I didn’t mention seasonality. HIVE has only been public for a few years, but have a look at June and July.

Returns are hugely positive for this time of year, lending credence to the idea that if you’re going to own HIVE, you really couldn’t select a better time. The opposite is true of August and September, so that’s something to watch for, but the chart and seasonality are lining up nicely.

Let’s kick things off with a look at where estimates stand.

The last three revisions have been down, which is obviously not something we want. I will say, however, that when I covered HIVE in January, estimates for 2027 were for just 27 cents in earnings; that’s gone all the way to 41 cents today. Similarly, estimates for 2025 have risen from a loss of 24 cents to a loss of 16 cents. Revenue estimates have exploded higher as well, with 2026 sales rising from $221M to $360M today. In other words, unless these estimates are just nowhere close to reality, HIVE has gotten enormously cheaper in the last five months.

HIVE has now also passed 10EH/s in peak hashrate. The company expects 11.5EH/s by the end of this month, on its way to 25EH/s by the end of its Q4.

In addition to ramping to 25EH/s, efficiency is set to rise pretty significantly as well.

Today’s efficiency is something like 18 J/TH, but it should improve to 16.5 later this year. That helps reduce mining costs, which boosts mining margins.

This table is illustrative of what HIVE’s mining operations could look like once it’s fully built out at 25EH/s. At the current Bitcoin price, HIVE expects mining margin of about $330M annually. At $150K per Bitcoin, it’s more like $575M. HIVE’s margins are enormously dependent upon the price of Bitcoin, as is any company that mines anything (gold, silver, copper, etc.). If you’re bullish on HIVE, you are bullish on Bitcoin.

As we’re looking at those mining margin numbers, let’s remind ourselves that HIVE’s market cap is $300M as of Friday’s close.

We can see below that relative to other miners, HIVE is tiny today. That’s set to change as we reach the end of September, assuming HIVE’s 25EH/s capacity comes online as expected.

Other miners are growing as well, but HIVE has the most relative growth coming based upon current info. Mining is a relative game, so HIVE is going to be much more competitive in a few months than it is today.

The other piece of the equation here is that the company’s HPC and AI growth is set to be outstanding in the coming months.

Annual recurring revenue in Q1 was $15M, but is set to be $100M by the end of this fiscal year. If HIVE gets anywhere near that, again, that’s a great growth lever for a stock with a $300M market cap.

Is It Really That Cheap?

HIVE’s valuation, as I mentioned above, has gotten much better. That’s true if you’re using 2027 EPS estimates, or if you’re looking at price-to-sales and tangible book value. Let’s start with P/S.

Today, shares are at 1.16X forward sales. Its three-year average has been 2.56X, which is obviously a 120% premium to today’s price. I don’t see how there is any conclusion I can draw from this chart other than the stock is cheap.

In addition, we can use tangible book value, and it’s unbelievably cheap there as well.

The most recent TBV reported was $3.10 against a share price of $1.89. As we can see below, shares at 0.61X TBV is well below the 1.51X average.

In conclusion, HIVE is absolutely very cheap. If growth plans in both Bitcoin mining and HPC come to fruition, HIVE should be even cheaper in the coming months. With a bullish setup in the chart, and what I view as a favorable fundamental backdrop aligned with a cheap valuation, I’m reiterating my strong buy rating on HIVE.

data.title: Palomar: A High-Growth Insurer At A Reasonable Price
data.description:
For a quality stock like Palomar Holdings (PLMR) a ~10% from the top as we are seeing now is possibly the best entry we can get if we are looking for fresh investments now. The next best entry was anytime in the past - such has been its momentum in the past few months, and for reasons.

Quality Growth With Sound Drivers

Although tapering a bit, revenue growth is resilient and extremely strong at over 45% YoY as of Q1 2025. PLMR is in a scaling phase, far from what we can assess. Growth rates during scaling up are expected to be high and some deceleration while it matures is not a concern - not when growth rates are this strong.

The once flagship earthquake segment is now reducing its contribution to overall GWP, with diversification into Crop and Inland Marine. Casualty products are gaining most recent traction. That augurs well for diversifying risks.

The topline has room to run. Earthquake remains underpenetrated in CA. Plus, now there are newer underpenetrated markets that is being addressed by PLMR. Tailwinds like climate volatility and insurer retrenchment from complex risks support PLMR's positioning. This shift widens its TAM and reduces risk concentration, supporting sustained revenue growth as it scales not only with new moat products but across geographies in the US. CA contributed 42% of total GWP a year ago and now accounts for 31%, showing PLMR's rapid expansion beyond CA.

Climate volatility, while a risk factor for insurers, can actually work in favor of PLMR. As larger insurers pull back from catastrophe prone regions due to rising loss ratios, PLMR can fill these gaps with tailored, rightly priced products and strong reinsurance backing. This retreat by peers gives PLMR pricing power and room to grow its footprint and riding on its disciplined underwriting track record.

Q1 2025 reported GWP growth of ~20%, further supporting revenue in upcoming quarters. I also like the same store growth rate of 37%. PLMR's GWP growth isn’t just a backward looking metric. It reflects broader strategic shifts that are still unfolding. The continued ramp in newer product lines and geographies, plus operational leverage from recent acquisitions, gives confidence that strong topline momentum can persist in the near term. Scalable strategies on digital distributions also make customer acquisition sustainable as PLMR expands.

PLMR's recent acquisitions add support to its topline growth potential by opening up new market segments and strengthening its diversification strategy. The acquisition of First Indemnity of America, a surety carrier focused on contract bonds for small and mid-sized contractors, opens up a stable, high-margin space that’s less correlated to broader P&C trends. PLMR can now use its own surety licenses to scale this segment nationally. Similarly, its acquisition of Advanced AgProtect, gives PLMR a foothold in multi peril crop, hail and livestock insurance - a specialized segment with deep local relationships and steady demand. These moves not only expand Palomar’s addressable market but also reduce geographic and product concentration.

Underwriting And Risk Transfer

Palomar shows robust underwriting numbers in Q1 2025, with a loss ratio of 23.6% and a combined ratio of 73.1%, both improving YoY. The Catastrophe loss ratio showed a net benefit (–0.3%), attributed to strong reinsurance recoveries. Its adjusted combined ratio came in even lower at 68.5%, emphasizing underwriting profitability. Underwriting income rose ~77% YoY despite higher expansion into newer lines and geographies.

Fair Valuations Indicating Upside

To analyze valuations, I am using P/B and forward P/E ratios and comparing against Kinsale Capital and RLI. PLMR is mostly trading below peers in terms of valuation. PLMR's current multiples show a company in the midst of a market re-rating (while both Kinsale and RLI have seen their valuations plateau) Although PLMR's valuation expansion is near historical highs, it still remains below Kinsale’s premium and roughly in line with RLI's, suggesting there may be further room to run if growth and profitability momentum persists.

Importantly, the rise in PLMR’s valuation doesn’t seem disconnected from the improving fundamentals. It likely reflects growing investor confidence in how PLMR is evolving - tightening underwriting and focusing on diversified specialty lines (moat like tailwinds in under-penetrated categories). If Palomar continues to improve its balance sheet and expand in catastrophe light segments, the current valuation could be the beginning of a shift, not the peak.

To put things in perspective, RLI reported a ~13% increase in gross written premiums (against ~20% for PLMR) in Q1 2025 and a combined ratio of 82.3 (against ~70% for PLMR). Corresponding numbers for Kinsale show only ~8% growth in gross premiums written and a combined ratio of 82.1. While both RLI and Kinsale are larger in scale, PLMR is growing faster off a smaller base while improving underwriting margins. It is still in a growth and efficiency expansion phase, whereas its peers are in more mature or cyclical stages. The scale up potential and profitability efficiencies back PLMR’s rising valuation multiples.

Differences in RoE are also indicative of PLMR's superior thesis. With an adjusted RoE of 27% in Q1 2025, PLMR reported a far higher RoE than RLI's comprehensive earnings RoE at ~20% and Kinsale RoE of ~23%. This despite being the growth phase where it does not have the scale to leverage capital efficiency.

Buy PLMR - Great Risk Reward

Before concluding, let us look at the PEG ratio for PLMR and where it sites in relation to established peers. For EPS growth estimates, I have calculated the CAGR of FY 2025 and FY 2026 YoY growth estimates in Seeking Alpha. KNSL's expected EPS growth till FY 2026 end is ~12.5%. This translates to a PEG ratio of ~2.16, potentially because of its stable business maturity and fundamentals. PEG for RLI's EPS growth of ~5%, translates to an even higher PEG ratio of 4.9. In contrast, PLMR's EPS CAGR comes out to be ~27.5%. That is a PEG of 0.82.

This significant divergence suggests that PLMR may be undervalued relative to its growth potential. Despite trading at a relatively elevated P/E multiple, the PEG framework helps understand why the premium may be more than justified given its high expected earnings trajectory.

Overall, what seems to be a valuation concern for PLMR (given its recent run-up) is actually still a great place to enter the stock. Fundamentals around topline growth and underwriting discipline are visibly strong. Tailwinds from under penetrated niche markets are long-term growth supportive. Additionally, valuations have not factored in the growth potential in earnings fully. Even if the valuations don't expand and PLMR delivers a part of the expected growth rates on EPS, there is meaningful upside to be had on earnings compounding alone.

Applying a 22x multiple to projected FY 2026 EPS of ~$8.32 supports a conservative 12–18 month price target of ~$185. If growth rates do materialize, then rerating could push up upsides again by another 10% at a minimum (above $200). That is a decent upside potential with lower earnings risk due to PLMR's proven underwriting quality. Buy PLMR for a minimum of 12-18 months for a conservative target of $200.

data.title: SMH: Not A Time To Be Greedy - Wait For Dips
data.description:
SMH Remains Compelling, Thanks To The Multi-Year AI Spending Trends, Albeit Warranting A Moderate Pullback

We previously covered VanEck Semiconductor ETF (NASDAQ:SMH) in April 2025, discussing how the ongoing tariff/ trade war and the higher recessionary risks might trigger a painful "wave of order cancellations through the supply chain," contributing to the ETF's steep selloff.

On the other hand, the ETF had remained attractive then due to the top three holdings' cheap valuations and oversold status, compared to their historical trends, significantly aided by their undisputed leadership in the semiconductor industry, their robust long-term fundamentals, and likely to be temporal pain, pending regulatory clarity.

SMH 1Y Stock Price

Since then, SMH has outperformed with a +46.2% rally as the bulls also defended the $170s floor during the worst of the April 2025 market wide meltdown, with a similar price performance also observed in its ETF and semiconductor stock peers in varying degrees.

This is significantly aided by numerous market players signaling the durability of the AI-related boom in their recent earnings calls, including hyperscalers, quantum computing companies, AI SaaS companies, and even data center REITs, with it further underscoring the robust semiconductor spending trends ahead.

The same has been reported by SMH's top three holdings, namely Nvidia (NVDA), Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM), and Broadcom (AVGO), with their recent Q1'25 earning calls delivering double beat and raised guidance performances while naturally allaying market fears about any spending slowdowns.

For example, NVDA reports data center revenues of $39.1B in FQ1'26 (-9.8% QoQ/ +73% YoY) while still reporting a relatively decent adj gross profit margins of 61% (-12.5 points QoQ/ -17.9 YoY/ -0.7 from FY2019 levels of 61.7%), despite missing out on the $2.5B in additional H20 revenues attributed to the new export requirements.

This is while guiding an excellent FQ2'26 revenue guidance of $45B (+2.1% QoQ/ +50% YoY), after factoring in a hefty $8B in H20 revenue loss arising from the export limitations, which otherwise may have boosted its FQ2'26 adj growth rates to +13.8% QoQ/ +76.6% YoY instead.

The same has been reported by AVGO, with FQ2'25 AI semiconductor revenues of $4.4B (+7.3% QoQ/ +46% YoY) as more hyperscalers adopt its custom AI accelerators for improved cost/ inferencing performance ratio, building upon its AI networking growth by +170% YoY as demand for new AI-based capabilities grow.

This is on top of the management's promising FQ3'25 AI semiconductor revenue guidance of $5.1B (+15.9% QoQ/ +64.5% YoY), with it underscoring the hyperscalers' insatiable demand for custom AI accelerators as they also reiterated/ raised their FY2025 data center CapEx plans in the recent Q1'25 earning calls.

These reasons may also be why TSM has been able to report a robust YTD revenue growth at +42.6% YoY and richer gross profit margins of 58.7% as of FQ1'25 (-0.3 points QoQ/ +5.7 YoY/ +12.7 from FY2019 levels of 46%).

Much of its tailwinds are thanks to its undeniable position as the world's largest foundry with a market share of 67% as of Q4'24 (+3 points QoQ/ +6 YoY) along with a "market share of 34% in "Foundry 2.0" industry, which TSMC defines as all logic wafer manufacturing, packaging, testing, mask-making and others, by unleashing customers' innovations and nurturing customers' success, which formed the foundation the Company's future success."

These reasons are also why the TSM management has felt confident to offer a promising FQ2'25 revenue guidance of $28.8B (+12.8% QoQ/ +38.3% YoY) and gross profit margins of 58% (-0.7 points QoQ/ +4.8 YoY).

Combined with the ETF's top three holdings' raised consensus forward estimates over the next few years, we can understand why market sentiments surrounding the semiconductor industry has lifted as it has, with it naturally boosting SMH's price outperformance thus far.

So, Is SMH Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

SMH 5Y Stock Price

For now, SMH has already charted an impressive recovery by +54.6% since the 52 weeks bottom in April 2025, with the ETF appearing to retest the prior resistance levels of $260s upon AVGO's highly promising earnings call a week ago.

This is significantly aided by the ongoing de-escalation in the trade war between the US/ China, building upon the prior 90-day tariff pause and the robust semiconductor prospects arising from the multi-year AI and quantum related spending trends.

SMH Nearing Overbought Position

These are causes for celebrations, indeed, as observed in the recovering market sentiments from prior pessimistic levels to current greedy levels.

On the other hand, we are not certain if it makes sense for anyone to chase SMH at current levels, as observed in the elevated Money Flow Index & Williams %R Momentum indexes above, with historical trading trends between April–August 2024 highlighting a potential reversal in price momentum ahead.

This is especially since the ETF's short interest float remains elevated at 11.4%, with its recent gains potentially moderated by aggressive short sellers and the ETF likely to retrace to the prior support levels of $235s and $250s ranges, implying a -7.8% downside (at the midpoint) from current levels.

SMH's Relatively Expensive Valuations

This is worsened by SMH's relatively elevated LTM P/E ratio of 40.25x (based on Trading View's last closing price and the LTM earnings per share), compared to the sector average of 38.51x and its ETF peers, including:

  • SPDR S&P Semiconductor ETF (NYSEARCA:XSD) at 38.75x,
  • iShares Semiconductor ETF (NASDAQ:SOXX) at 36.38x,
  • SPDR S&P 500 ETF (NYSEARCA:SPY) at 32.07x,
  • and Nasdaq (NASDAQ:QQQ) at 38.21x,

with it implying a reduced margin of safety at current levels.

The same elevated valuations have also been observed in NVDA's FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 33.40x compared to the recent bottom of 20.85x, TSM at 22.85x/ 13.11x and AVGO at 38.28x/ 21.21x, respectively, with it underscoring why the recent rally may have occurred overly fast and furious.

SMH's Established Support Levels At 100/ 200 Day Moving Averages

As a result, while we may maintain our Buy rating for the SMH ETF, attributed to the bright long-term prospects of its three top holdings, it comes with the caveat that investors wait for a moderate retracement to its 100/ 200 day moving averages for an improved margin of safety, as similarly highlighted in the above stock price chart.

data.title: Murphy USA: Pullback Creates Opportunity (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Murphy USA (MUSA) have been a disappointing performer over the past year, losing 15% of their value amid concerns about consumer spending at its convenience stores (C-stores). I last covered shares of Murphy in early April when I downgraded the stock to a hold on valuation grounds. While I was right to downgrade shares, I should have moved them to sell, as the stock has lost 13% while the market has gained over 10%, a jarring amount of underperformance. Shares are now at my $400-420 buy range. Given this performance and with updated financials, now is a time to see if investors should buy the dip or if fundamentals have deteriorated enough to make now unattractive.

Problems accelerated for MUSA shareholders after the company reported very disappointing first quarter earnings on May 7th, earning just $2.63, $1.33 behind estimates. This was down from $3.12 a year ago. This was a disappointing quarter. Indeed, EBITDA declined by $7 million to $154 million. While core sales were flattish, operating expenses caused a larger than expected pressure on operating margins.

Looking first at revenue trends, gasoline volumes were down 1.9% to 1.13 billion gallons and down 4.2% on a same-store basis. There was one fewer day in the quarter, which would be a ~1.2% headwind. Additionally, this year had a harsher winter, leading to a 50bp drag from increased store closures. Otherwise, volumes would have been flattish.

While the calendar day factor is entirely fair, I view the winter weather commentary as not a meaningful driver of lower gasoline sales. As you can see, up until April gasoline volumes tracked right in-line with last year’s results, suggesting there was no meaningful impact. If anything, the relative comparison is more concerning today than in Q1. Over the past four weeks, gasoline demand has lagged last year’s level. The weather in the Northeast has been unseasonably cold, which may be reducing beach travel, but it will be essential to see demand pick up as we exit June and enter the peak of the summer driving season.

One offsetting bright spot was that Murphy made 25.4 cents per gallon (“cpg”), up from 24.8 a year ago. Still while up from last year, this was a bit weaker than expected, given the excess supply of refined products. Wider margins and lower sales broadly offset, and as a result, fuel margin was flat at $287 million. I am slightly more encouraged about the potential accretion in margins than volume in Q2. We have flipped from having excess inventory of gasoline three months ago to below-normal inventories today, which should support margins and MUSA’s pricing power.

One new variable is the burgeoning Israel-Iran conflict, which caused a knee-jerk surge in crude oil prices, and there are likely to be at least temporary supply outages as a result of the conflict. How long the conflict will last is impossible to know. However, MUSA tends to generate higher fuel margins during periods of price volatility. As such, an increase in volatility is likely to be an earnings positive. However, a material surge in crude prices past $90, would likely start to have negative consequences on volume and the real economy. Volatility in the $60-90 range will likely be positive for MUSA’s results, but increases beyond $90 would start to become a drag.

Beyond fuel trends, the bigger concern for me is activity inside the c-stores. Merchandise sales were down slightly at $999 million, a fairly disappointing outcome, even adjusting for a fewer calendar days. Nicotine same store sales were down about 2.5% while non-nicotine same store sales were up just under 1%. Now, merchandise contribution rose by 2.3% to $196 million. Margins expanded by 40bps to 19.6%, more than offsetting lower volume. About 45% of sales come from nicotine products.

Historically, as you can see below, c-store demand holds up well even during economic downturns. Indeed, on the earnings call, management highlighted that results are benefitting from consumers trading down. Middle-income ($55k-$100k) customers are now half of its loyalty program from below 40% at launch. A revamped menu and loyalty program increased QuickChek sandwich sales by 8%. Offsetting this, there were fewer lottery ticket sales given smaller jackpots. With Mega Millions moving to a $5 ticket from $2 previously, that could be a headwind to volumes going forward, though it is supposed to lead to larger jackpots more frequently.

Now while, c-store demand has typically held up well during a recession, sales at convenience stores have declined 4% from last year. MUSA has outperformed this trend, consistent with its store refurbishment leading to market share gains. For instance, nicotine volumes are essentially flat since 2021, even as the industry is down 20%. I expect the US to avoid recession, so while sale growth may not accelerate meaningfully, I do not expect declines.

The other major headwind has been staffing cost. Store operating expenses rose by $14 million to $266 million; expenses rose by 5.7% due to higher wage and maintenance costs. With sales flat but costs up, EBITDA declined as noted earlier. Here, a little softening of the labor market can be a good thing, tamping down on wage growth and reducing vacancy, allowing MUSA to expand store hours. There is already evidence the market is loosening. Staff applications are double last year and back above pre-COVID levels. This should support slowing expense growth.

At the same time, MUSA continues its solid capital return program. It did $151 million of buybacks, and there has been 4.5% share count reduction over the past year. $788 million of buyback authorization remains, and management aims to cut its share count by 15% by 2028. MUSA has $2 billion of debt for 2x debt to EBITDA leverage, a very reasonable level.

Alongside results, MUSA continues to offer solid guidance. Given my view the US will not have a recession, I view this guidance as credible. If oil prices do move above $90, I see downside risk to fuel volumes. Plus, that pressure on discretionary income would also likely be a headwind for merchandise results of 1-3%.

MUSA’s share price decline is an example of what can happen when a company with a full valuation delivers imperfect results—shares can get disproportionately impacted. I see MUSA still generating at least $400 million of free cash flow this year, assuming summer diving volumes accelerate as I expect.

With ongoing buybacks, I expect the share count to be below 19.2 million by year-end or $20-21 of run-rate free cash flow. I view a 5% free cash flow yield as attractive, given its relative lack of cyclicality and the durability of the c-store model, as well as ongoing market share gains from smaller operators with higher costs. As such, I am moving shares back to a “buy” and would use this relative underperformance to get back into MUSA.

data.title: Double Trouble: 2 BDCs Facing Another Yield Cut, Time To Sell?
data.description:
As many of my closest followers have probably noticed, I have become more skeptical about the near- to medium-term BDC (business development company) return prospects. Lower base rates, higher long-term yields, shallow LBO/M&A volumes, and a growing supply of new private credit actors have introduced significant pressure on BDC earnings across the board.

However, this does not mean that I have turned bearish. It is more that I am trying to be quite vocal here on the Seeking Alpha platform in terms of emphasizing the importance of sticking to top-tier BDCs with robust and resilient fundamentals. I simply do not see a positive case for BDCs that are currently walking on thin ice, where investors could potentially bet on profiting from sudden positive external shocks.

In other words, currently, it is not the right time to be aggressive on BDCs.

One potential way someone could approach the selection of sustainable income-producing BDCs is to focus on BDCs that have already registered dividend cuts. The idea is that the post-dividend cut yields should have a higher margin of safety and low probabilities of being cut again; as, theoretically, the Management, when making the decision to reduce it, should have already factored in some cushion for weathering any incremental challenges.

There is definitely logic behind it.

Yet, I would not take this nuance for granted.

I will now give you two practical cases, which will illustrate the point.

#1 - TriplePoint Venture Growth

TriplePoint Venture Growth (TPVG) is a venture capital-focused BDC. It means that the businesses in which TPVG invests have not yet achieved sustainable operations and cash flows. Instead, these businesses are typically still in a go-to-market strategy-shaping period and really are trying to get some first meaningful traction for their products.

In practice, all of this just increases the overall risk level to which BDC/TPVG investors are exposed—and yes, this goes on top of having already a high baseline risk.

Now, due to the formation of negative headwinds in the BDC sector (such as those that I outlined above) and the materialization of TPVG-specific risks, the Management announced a well-needed dividend cut in mid-2024. The base dividend was reduced from $0.40 per share to $0.30 per share.

This is a rather notable correction and, theoretically, implies that TPVG should now be able to enjoy higher cash retention levels.

What is interesting is that, currently, even after the cut, TPVG's yield is still one of the highest in the sector — at 16.3%.

For me, this fact alone is already a huge red flag.

Let me now give you the three most important reasons why I think that TPVG's dividend revision process is not over.

First, even after the cut, the dividend coverage is negative. If we take the Q1, 2025 earnings of $0.27 per share, we arrive at a base dividend coverage of 90%. Namely, TPVG has to use debt to close the gap, which is certainly not sustainable.

Second, it is not only the dividend yield that is high here, but also the overall portfolio debt investment yield is among the highest in the BDC sector. And what we can see already for several quarters in a row is how the portfolio yield has been gradually declining towards the current market-level yields. There is about 200 basis points of a gap, which is very significant. So, I do not see how TPVG could deliver NII per share growth while having these headwinds in place.

Third, we should not rule out additional non-accruals, especially knowing what is happening in the economy. Again, as I articulated in the intro section above, this is not the right time to be aggressive. By going long TPVG, investors are automatically assuming rather aggressive risk levels. If there are really new non-accruals emerging, the second iteration of the dividend cut could come as soon as Q3 or Q4 this year.

#2 - Oaktree Specialty Lending

Oaktree Specialty Lending (NASDAQ:OCSL) is another BDC that has made a dividend cut, but even with that in mind, the sustainability of the current yield looks questionable.

The dividend cut was made in February 2025, when the quarterly base amount was decreased from $0.50 per share to $0.40 per share. So, the dividend was cut by 27%.

Currently, the dividend yield stands at ~11%, which is more realistic (from the sustainability point of view) than for TPVG's.

Having said that, I think that the actual situation is worse than it might seem from the surface.

OCSL just barely covers the dividend. Based on the recent earnings report, the dividend coverage is 113%. It might seem like a solid level, but actually, if we adjust for the temporary incentive fee waivers, we would get almost 100% coverage (e.g., in Q2, 2025, the waived amount accounted for ~12% of the total adjusted NII per share). Namely, the margin of safety is still extremely low.

To make matters worse, it is not like OCSL has a growing NII per share level. Similar to TPVG, OCSL has a higher debt portfolio yield than could be obtained in the market. So, there is some negative convergence process left to recognize. Plus, in early 2027, OCSL will have to refinance $350 million of 3.5% yielding debt, which will almost certainly cost more (based on the current yield curve, the new level would be double the existing one).

Finally, if I had to name the single biggest risk or potential driver for further declining NII per share generation, it would be the non-accrual component, which has been there for many quarters in a row. For instance, in the most recent financial quarters, OCSL wrote off $0.97 per share of its NAV. It is very hard to predict how many and how meaningfully new non-accruals will emerge, but one thing is for sure—we have to factor in such a probability.

Now, if we combine all of these aspects together, I, frankly, do not see how OCSL's yield could be deemed sustainable.

The bottom line

In this article, I have shared why it is crucial to still pay detailed attention to the underlying fundamentals and cash flows when determining the sustainability of BDC yields even after dividend cuts have been made.

A recent dividend cut does not automatically guarantee a sustainable yield going forward.

The two BDCs I have discussed above serve as great examples of this principle.

data.title: Intel: Even A Giant Leap Of Faith May Not Be Enough
data.description:
Intel: No Magic Turnaround as Stock Underperforms

If you are a "diamond hand" Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC) investor, I can empathize with you, as you awaited with baited breadth on whether the market could give the benefit of the doubt to CEO Lip-Bu Tan's multi-year transformation journey. Notwithstanding Tan's undisputed semiconductor pedigree, reigniting the fortunes of the former King of semis is easier said than done.

Tan didn't mince his words, as he unleashed a series of bureaucratic cuts of Intel's middle layer to better streamline its cost efficiencies. With Intel's previous bloated cost base, the move isn't only critical, but also foundational toward helping INTC bridge the gap with the leading semiconductor peers: Nvidia (NVDA), AMD (AMD), and TSMC (TSM).

Yet, with INTC underperforming its semi-peers (SMH) since reaching its highs in early 2025, I believe the early indications suggest that the reception is far from forthcoming. It's notable that the leading trio in the VanEck Semiconductor ETF have either inched close to or even surpassed (in the case of Broadcom (AVGO)) their 2025 zenith, demonstrating the palpable lack of confidence in Tan's revival thesis. In INTC's case, the stock remains in the doldrums, hugging the lows close to the $20 zone, and substantially below the 2025 peak of $27 struck in February this year.

In my previous INTC article, I articulated why the company faces highly complex structural, geopolitical, and technological hurdles that suggest optimism of a quick fix by Tan is arguably not justified. Since Tan assumed the position as Intel's head honcho, he has delivered two key conferences (Intel Vision, and Intel Foundry Direct Connect) to promulgate the performance metrics and strategy that Intel will embark on to (hopefully) overturn its disappointing performance.

Yet, there seems to be little interest (for now) from Tan to set about the separation of Intel Foundry and its design/products business. The leadership under Tan's direction seems to be committed to fulfilling its strategic roadmap but differentiated with supposedly "sharper" execution to meet its objectives.

Okay... I'm not doubting Tan's ability to carry out the required actions to lift Intel Foundry from its underperformance while navigating its CapEx roadmap to compete more effectively with TSMC. However, the fact that TSMC has been a clear winner in the AI race, winning firm orders from Nvidia, demonstrates that the bifurcation between TSMC and Intel appears to be widening.

Intel believes that its retrofitted 18A and 18A-P processes can help justify subsequent volume production, as it aims to sign up more external customers. Its strategic capacity allocation (70% internal and 30% external) has also provided investors an opportunity to benchmark the company's progress, although the lack of firm capacity commitments suggests that undertaking the advancement to 14A could prove to be more challenging than anticipated.

And with Intel well behind Nvidia and AMD in the AI chips race, it remains to be seen just how fast (and far) can Tan revive Intel's execution, while contending with a rapidly evolving NVDA, as it seeks to broaden its ecosystem advantages. AMD is also assessed to be closing the technological gap with Nvidia, although Nvidia's CUDA libraries are expected to help firmly entrench its dominance over AMD's forays. Yet, the fast-growing AI TAM suggests that AMD doesn't need to capture the lion's share of the market opportunity to lift its growth cadence.

Herein lies the main challenge for Intel. Obviously, the company must continue to invest in CapEx to make sure it has the necessary capacity to meet its own requirements while aiming to grow its external footprint. Management has emphasized the need to streamline "new" investments while optimizing its current commitments, assuring investors that it will not invest for the sake of investing to catch up with TSMC.

Yet, with Intel far behind Nvidia (for AI chips), and TSMC (for foundry leadership and capacity) in their respective segments, it must still battle AMD in the data center CPU business, which appears to continue trending in favor of the Lisa Su-led company based on recent results. Moreover, with Arm (ARM) notably strong in the cloud service providers' opportunity, Intel is facing significant impediments in taking on AMD (for on-premise migration) and Arm (for CSP) concurrently, while attempting to challenge Nvidia and TSMC for new business. In other words, I believe it only makes sense for the company to seriously contemplate splitting its foundry and products business to have a realistic chance of overturning its deficits and gaps that have left it in the current shambolic state.

Intel: Not Cheap and Not Expected To Be Profitable Anytime Soon

Not surprisingly, analysts aren't enthused with Intel's recent overtures, even as Tan went on a charm offensive with the investing and analyst community. As INTC continues to underperform its semi-peers, the market is likely concerned about whether it makes sense to continue placing their faith in what could turn out to be another negative FCF situation through the end of 2026. With the AI landscape rapidly gaining traction in favor of Nvidia, while TSMC is mopping up the manufacturing partnership, could it take too long for Intel to be ready before the digestion of AI infra capacity begins in earnest?

I think these are valid and justified questions that Intel investors must ask while assessing whether its current valuation multiples have considered most of these headline risks.

INTC is valued at a forward EBITDA multiple of 9.1x, above its 10Y average of about 8x. I don't think I need to emphasize to you that the stock isn't priced at a discount, much less a steep one, to be aggressive dip buyers at the current levels. In other words, investing in INTC now requires a giant leap of faith in the execution prowess of Tan, while allowing more time for Intel to restructure its business quickly enough to regain the confidence of the market.

While I've examined that INTC's price action has been consolidating within the $19 and $26 support zone, the lack of buying fervor amidst the current recovery lifting SMH intimates the astonishing lack of confidence in Tan's roadmap.

It goes to show that the market is seeking clearer answers on how Intel can maintain its relevance as AI growth drivers become increasingly critical to overcome typical semiconductor cyclicality. In addition, with TSMC maintaining its tight grip on AI chip manufacturing and advanced packaging, it seems inconceivable to think that Intel could somehow conjure something out of the magic hat to win enough customers to gain traction toward meeting the roadmap to justify its 14A node.

Hence, the market's desire to seek proof points to validate Intel's turnaround journey will likely see the stock continuing to trade within this range in the foreseeable future. Unless you are an investor enamored with sideways consolidation, I exhort you to think carefully about whether staying on board with INTC still makes much sense.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NuScale Power: Tailwinds Are Here, But Elevated Costs Weigh
data.description:
Well, I better be the first to admit I may have been misinformed and off on my prediction for NuScale Power (NYSE:SMR) back in February. The stock was trading around $18 a share and I gave the firm a sell rating. Three-plus months later, SMR is riding high trading at $41. I own up to my missed call, and it’s time to assess where I went wrong and why the stock has generated some hefty momentum.

SMR is a provider of advanced small modular reactor nuclear technology. Through a small, pressurized water reactor, their products can generate between 77 megawatts and 924 Mwe of total output. Small nuclear reactors are being considered and studied more in recent years, as the nuclear industry advances and these products are much smaller in space and can be less complex than large nuclear reactors. On the other hand, they are very costly and even an industry frontrunner like SMR has had a challenging time securing an operational reactor to this point. After recent news and tailwind signs for SMR, I am rating the stock a hold as I am still leery on the expenses and finalization of operating small nuclear reactors.

NRC Approval

Of the positive forces driving SMR, the approval from the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission [NRC] for NuScale’s design of a 462-MW small modular reactor power plant, is arguably their biggest recent win. The power uprate is 77 Mwe for each power module and is the second approved small modular reactor for the company. No other firm in this industry has had any approval from the NRC to this point, putting SMR as the clear commander in this field. This decision even came earlier than expected, catching investors by surprise with excitement. Joining their 50 Mwe design that was approved a few years ago, the new design is 76’ x 15’ per module and has a capacity factor of 95%.

Hopeful on Customer

NuScale does not have a real customer deal in place yet, but in the Q1 2025 earnings they indicated confidence in a “firm customer order” by year-end 2025. SMR is partnered with ENTRA1 Energy as their exclusive commercialization partner. There are advanced discussions occurring with data centers, utilities, and coal plant operators looking at beginning or transitioning to small nuclear. But, even if a deal gets finalized soon, the power plant would still likely not be operational until the end of 2030. Despite the potentially great news of a first “real” customer, NuScale even as the industry leader, is still years away from an up and running plant.

Trump and Nuclear

The president has recently announced a few executive orders that are aimed to benefit the nuclear power sector, which has been another driving tailwind for SMR. He wants to expand American nuclear capacity from around 100 GW today to 400 GW by 2050. Of course, this is fantastic news for nuclear companies. Trump has geared orders toward national security, by authorizing deployment of advanced reactors for AI data centers and military bases. The president is also instructing the NRC to speed up their regulatory process in approval of reactors, ideally within 18 months of submissions.

Financials

Revenue exploded for SMR in Q4 2024 hitting $34.2 million, up from $4.6 million Q4 2023. Q1 2025 revenue was $13.4 million. The recent escalation is chiefly due to revenues generated from the company’s RoPower project in Romania. This agreement is through Flour Corporation, the majority investor in SMR, helping them with procurement. The deal is to provide RoPower Nuclear with design and engineering services for the implementation of a project.

SMR is not yet profitable and really is still a long way off. Operating expenses remain high and net income was still $136.62 million in the red for Q4 2024. On the balance sheet, SMR is fine, assets outweigh liabilities, and they support a current ratio of 6.23.

Costs Weigh Heavy

I noted in my prior article on SMR, that costs for construction and operation of small nuclear reactors are incredibly high. Per megawatt, models have shown greater than 50% cost for small nuclear reactors versus the traditional large reactors. Outside of regulations and approvals, this is the clear next biggest roadblock for the global industry, and why there are only three operating small reactor plants worldwide. The three that are running in China and Russia are also at a 300-400% cost overrun.

SMR made an attempt at the first small nuclear plant in the U.S. in 2023 with the Utah Associated Municipal Power System. Even after initial agreements, the deal fell through as expenses rose and reached over $20 million per megawatt. Total project cost increased by 3x to an expected over $9.3 billion before it was finally shut down.

Even in the large reactor space, which is knowingly less expensive, the Vogtle plant in Georgia is the only U.S. plant built in decades here. This project is known as the “most expensive power plant” ever built on earth. In a power comparison, nuclear has become more expensive to be operational. Other sources like solar and wind have become cheaper, while fossil fuels are the steady leader that trudges along on high demand.

Conclusion

The 120% gain in the last month is riding on the coattails of several internal and industry tailwinds. Their second approval from the NRC puts them in the driver seat in the small nuclear reactor industry. The company is optimistic and landing their first binding deal by this year, and the President’s demands on nuclear growth can help in years to come.

At the end of the day, they are a long way from being profitable and still do not have a deal in place with any customers to this point. Costs involved in small nuclear construction and operations are still a threat to the entire industry and have continued to prove as an issue. It’s hard to value a stock with negative earnings, but the climb in price has resulted in a price to sales of 86, astronomically high. Another interesting note is the 18% short interest the stock is now seeing from investors.

SMR has great momentum today but until an actual deal is in place and expenses are brought down, I am still going to be cautious, and is why I give the stock a hold rating.

data.title: If I Could Only Own 3 REITs, It Would Be These
data.description:
Sometimes my readers ask me:

What REITs would I buy if I owned only 3 of them?

I understand why I get this question so often.

Not everyone is ready to build a diversified REIT portfolio with over 20 names, as that would require a lot of upfront work to pick and choose the right REITs and constant monitoring to make sure they perform well over time.

Most people are not that interested in REITs (VNQ).

They would rather pick three solid REITs to get exposure to the sector and then spend their time on the golf course...

But answering that question is harder than simply picking my three top picks of the moment.

If I could only own three REITs, I would want to focus on large and resilient REITs with investment-grade rated balance sheets and solid long-term growth prospects.

Sun Communities, Inc. (SUI)

Sun Communities would be my pick for residential real estate.

It is a large and well-diversified REIT with a $16+ billion market cap and a very resilient balance sheet.

The REIT just recently sold its marina business to Blackstone (BX) and used a big chunk of the proceeds to pay off debt and buy back stock, leaving it with a very conservative 3x Debt-to-EBITDA, one of the lowest of any residential REITs.

It also left the REIT with an even more resilient portfolio.

The REIT owns manufactured housing communities and RV parks in growing markets. These are very desirable properties because their supply is strictly limited, but the demand for affordable housing is steadily growing. Moreover, as the landlord, SUI commonly owns only the land and associated infrastructure. The tenant then brings their own home or RV, which greatly reduces the need for capex. It also makes the tenant more dependent on your property, since moving a home is impractical and expensive.

The end result is steady high occupancy and faster rent growth:

Their business is doing very well today.

Occupancy reached 98.0% in Q1, up 150 basis points YoY, while same-property NOI rose 4.6%.

That's one of the highest occupancy and growth rates of any residential REIT today.

But despite having so little leverage, a resilient portfolio, and solid growth prospects, the REIT is still priced at a very reasonable valuation.

Its management issued post-disposition guidance of $6.53 in core FFO per share for this year, which puts its FFO multiple at 18.3x. That gives it about 20% upside to its long-term average multiple, but we think SUI likely deserves an even higher multiple due to its now lower leveraged balance sheet and higher quality, more focused portfolio post-marina sale.

Moreover, the dividend yield of 3.4% is near its highest level in many years after recently hiking it by 11%, and it is still well-covered by a very low payout ratio.

A 3.4% dividend yield coupled with high single-digit growth and some upside from multiple expansion should be enough to earn double-digit total returns for years to come.

The management has an amazing track record of market outperformance, and everything indicates that this outperformance is set to continue. The REIT is stronger than ever, and yet, its valuation is today historically low.

The recent deal to sell the marina business signifies that SUI's management is capable of backtracking and refocusing on its core competency when it benefits shareholders. They are not merely empire-building, and we love that.

Kite Realty Group Trust (KRG)

Another property type that I would want to own is grocery-anchored, service-oriented strip centers.

They are today undersupplied and enjoying solid rent growth, especially in the sunbelt markets that enjoy strong population and job growth:

Kite Realty owns one of the best portfolios in its peer group and has consistently enjoyed some of the fastest rent growth.

On top of that, it has one of the strongest balance sheets, with very low leverage, which makes it a safer choice for my REIT portfolio:

Despite the low leverage, resilient portfolio, and solid growth, the REIT is traded at one of the lowest valuations in its peer group today.

Kite Realty Group Retail Peers
P/FFO 10.5x ~15-17x
P/NAV 30% discount ~10% discount

This low valuation gives it a margin of safety, but also enjoys significant upside potential in the future as the market eventually recognizes its strength and it rerates at a higher multiple.

Why is it so cheap then?

Historically, KRG has had a riskier balance sheet, and it has owned a weaker portfolio. The REIT went through a major transformation in recent years, and this appears to have been overlooked by the market so far.

I am happy to buy a position, collect a 5% dividend yield, and wait patiently for the REIT to keep growing its cash flow, which will eventually be reflected in a higher valuation.

The upside potential is about 30% if and when it reprices at a valuation that's in line with its close peers.

EastGroup Properties, Inc. (EGP)

Finally, I would want to own some industrial properties in my REIT portfolio.

I expect this property sector to enjoy strong secular growth from the growing trends of e-commerce and onshoring, both of which will be boosted by the recent advances in AI and robotics.

E-commerce will accelerate because AI will lead to even stronger marketing and cheaper and faster shipping.

Onshoring will also benefit as robotics/AI will result in lower domestic manufacturing costs. This, coupled with tariffs, could significantly boost the demand for industrial properties.

EGP is one of the leading REITs specializing in urban, last-mile industrial properties in rapidly growing Sunbelt markets.

These properties enjoy high barriers to entry because they are often in infill locations that are already built out, but they are rapidly growing in demand, also benefiting from the strong population and job growth of their Sunbelt markets.

The REIT also has a very strong balance sheet with low debt and long debt maturities, making it resilient to higher interest rates.

Its management has an amazing track record of growing its funds from operations, or FFO, per share at a rapid pace by hiking its rents and developing new properties.

Its valuation is not particularly low today, trading at 18.5x FFO and a relatively low 3% yield, but its long runway of rapid growth potential easily justifies this multiple in my opinion.

The REIT has been a massive outperformer in the past, and I expect this outperformance to continue going forward.

Closing Note

If you decide to just own a few REITs, you are better off focusing on large growth-oriented REITs with strong investment-grade rated balance sheets.

These three REITs are good examples, but there are many others.

At High Yield Landlord, we invest primarily in this type of REIT, and we then supplement them with more speculative plays that offer higher yields and upside potential. They are, of course, riskier as well, but that is why we diversify.

data.title: Two Alternatives For A Potential $5000 Monthly Income
data.description:
Introduction:

The stock market is once again near its all-time highs. This is besides the fact that there was extreme panic just two months ago due to trade-related policies. Even though nothing much has changed on the surface. The deadline for the end of the 90-day pause of reciprocal tariffs is less than a month away. In fact, the tariffs on steel and aluminum have gone up from 25% to 50%. Further, there has been a confirmed trade deal with only one country so far, but sure, many are in the pipeline. So, something that may look to be outright scary to the market may not feel so after two months. The point we are trying to make is that the stock market is inherently volatile, and long-term investors should ignore the day-to-day noise. Instead, investors should focus on strategies that are diversified and have a record of delivering solid returns in good times and bad. Also, many investors tend to chase the hype around certain growth stocks in the hope of catching the next Apple (AAPL) or Google (GOOGL). Some people can do it successfully, but it does not work for the vast majority of investors. There is nothing bad about that as long as it is done with a small part of your portfolio while the majority of the capital is invested in more mature and dividend-paying companies. In that spirit, we will discuss two highly diversified income and growth focus strategies, which try to achieve the same goals with different approaches.

Why Do You Need Income?

Income is obviously very important to retirees. But what about anyone who is not retired? We believe the transition from a pure growth portfolio to an income portfolio should be gradual over many years. You cannot just do this transition overnight the day you retire. Anything that is not properly planned beforehand can run into pitfalls and cause unnecessary headaches if not monetary losses. We believe this transition should start at least 10 years in advance of the age at which you think you are going to retire. Even otherwise, everyone should have exposure to some solid blue-chip dividend-paying stocks. After all, from 1940 until 2024, a full 34% of the market returns can be attributed to reinvested dividends. It gets even better for the period of 1960 to 2024, when a full 44% return of the S&P500 came from the reinvested dividends. Also, when you add the power of compounding to the reinvested dividends, a full 85% of the returns of the S&P500 (from 1960 to 2024) can be attributed to these two factors.

Chart-1:

However, one needs to be cautious not to go for stocks with the highest yields, as they may be paying very high yields for a reason. One needs to look at both the yield and the dividend growth of the past few years. It is also advisable to have a mix of both of these types of stocks – one with high-growth but low starting yield, and the other with higher starting yield with mediocre growth. This is where the “Chowder number” comes in handy. This factor sums up the current yield of a stock and the past 5 years of dividend growth. If the total is 10 or higher, it is considered to be a good dividend stock. Obviously, this is not the only criterion that should go into the process of making buy/sell decisions.

(Note: ‘Chowder number’ was first made popular by a Seeking Alpha Author named Chowder).

More Than One Way to Achieve the Same Goals:

As they say, there is always more than one path to get to the same destination. This certainly applies to the world of investing. In this article, we will look at two methods (or portfolios) to generate up to 6% income and also provide decent growth over the long term. If possible, one should invest in multiple strategies, as no strategy performs equally well in different market conditions.

Here are our goals in brief.

Goals:

  1. Reasonably moderate income of 6% ($5,000 monthly on a $1 million investment)
  2. Decent growth to beat inflation
  3. Capital conservation
  4. Lower volatility and drawdowns.

Two Alternatives: Which One is Better?

In this article, we will present two alternative options to achieve the same goals. So, which one is better? It is hard to answer such questions with any degree of certainty, especially if we are talking about their performance in the future. However, we should analyze this on the basis of the suitability of one over the other based on individual circumstances. What may be perfect for one person may not suit another one. Our personal preference is always to diversify our bets, which means if it allows, one should divide one’s capital into more than one strategy. In other words, if the resources and time allow, one should invest in both options. We will discuss this in more detail once we have explained the two options in detail.

Option One:

In the first option, we will structure a fund-based portfolio with a set of 10 funds, each allocated 10%. This portfolio is a kind of set-it-and-forget-it type of portfolio. With these 10 funds selected to represent a wide array of asset classes, and each fund holding nearly 100 stocks or securities, we get a very nice diversification and relative safety. We also include a triple-A-rated CLO bond fund to provide nearly 6% income and low volatility. Also included is a gold-specific ETF to provide asset diversification and a hedge against inflation, though it provides no income.

The ten funds are: (ADX), (SCHD), (PEO), (JEPQ), (JEPI), (UTG), (RNP), (JAAA), (QQQ), and (GLDM).

Table-1: Portfolio (Option-1)

There are obviously pluses and minuses with any approach. So let’s see what they are with this portfolio:

Positive(s):

  1. Very easy to implement and maintain.
  2. Meets the income needs of 6%, which translates to $5,000 a month on an investment of $1 million.
  3. Reasonable growth over the long term, likely within the range of 80% to 120% of the S&P500.

Negative(s):

  1. An ongoing fee structure of 0.45%, which translates to $45 a year on every $10,000 capital invested. The investor does not pay this fee as such, but it is taken out of the fund’s returns. That being said, an average fee of 0.45% is not unreasonable overall, as some of these funds have active management and also provide managed distribution.
  2. Dependence on the continuity of quality management. For example, fund ADX has provided excellent performance in the past, and long term, it has even beaten the S&P500 at times, but a whole lot of the future performance depends on the continuity of the same quality of management in the future.

Option Two:

In the second option, we will use mostly individual stocks to generate the required income, supplemented by three ETFs to provide growth, diversification, and capital conservation. Overall, we think the net product is a very balanced portfolio, meeting most of our goals. Sure, it has some negatives as well, which we will discuss in a minute.

In the portfolio presented below, we use 10 individual stocks; a number of them are undervalued, and the downside risk is low. At the same time, all of them provide a healthy dividend yield of 5% to 9%. These 10 stocks make up 70% of the portfolio assets. In an effort to make the portfolio well diversified, we selected two ETFs, namely, Schwab U. S. Dividend ETF (SCHD) and Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ), with the additional benefit of providing the required capital growth. We also added a third ETF, Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF (JAAA), with the goal of providing steady income and capital conservation. Each of the three ETFs has been given a 10% allocation.

The stocks/securities are: (AES), (ARCC), (BTI), (CVX), (LYB), (MPLX), (O), (PFE), (RIO), (VZ), (JAAA), (SCHD), and (QQQ).

Table-2: Portfolio (Option-2)

Note: MPLX is a midstream partnership and provides a K-1 tax form (instead of 1099-Div). Please use due diligence.

Positive(s):

  1. Fairly easy to implement initially but will require some ongoing maintenance.
  2. Meets the income needs of 6% handily, which translates to $5,000 a month on an investment of $1 million.
  3. Reasonable growth over the long term, but may lag the S&P500.
  4. Very low fee structure. There is no cost to own the 10 stocks. The 3 ETFs are very low fees.

Negative(s):

  1. The stocks portion of this portfolio will require some maintenance. Any company that is fundamentally sound today may or may not be so after a few years. There can be a variety of factors that can impact the business and financials of a company. These include new competition, changes in govt. policies & regulations, and changes in management’s focus and effective use of the company’s capital, etc. So, any individual stock needs to be monitored at least quarterly or biannually to make sure that the company is still headed in the right direction. Of course, there is a ton of help and resources available online to research any stock, but it still needs some time and focus.
  2. In our choice of ETFs, at least one ETF, QQQ, being a growth-oriented fund, can also be highly volatile. To top it off, QQQ provides an almost insignificant income yield. However, we need to remember that the purpose of the fund is to provide growth, and the allocation is only 10%.

Backtesting Results of Two Portfolios:

Table-3: For the period from 2021 to May 2025

Note: The fund JEPQ was replaced with ETY, while JEPI was replaced with EVT.

Please note that the performance of the second portfolio is much lower, but it should be viewed from a different angle. Some of the stocks in the portfolio, such as AES, LYB, CVX, PFE, and VZ, are trading significantly lower and, in our view, undervalued at the current time. This is also reflected in the past performance metrics.

Now, the backtesting covering only five years is probably not good enough, so we removed JAAA from both portfolios as it does not have a history prior to 2020. After removing JAAA, we test them for a longer period of nearly 15 years.

Table-3B: For the period from 2012 to May 2025

Risk Factors:

  1. Both our portfolios have a majority of their investments in equities. There is very little allocation to bonds or fixed-income securities. Stocks or equities are, by nature, volatile and risky. They can lose significant value, sometimes as much as 50% or more, during panics and recessions. Investors should invest based on their tolerance for volatility and drawdowns.
  2. All investments are subject to geopolitical risks.
  3. Risk of high interest rates and a recession: The interest rates have been high for longer than expected. Also, the Fed has not indicated any inclination to reduce the rates in a hurry, even though the rates have been coming down in other economies around the world. This poses a risk to the continued growth of the economy. If the Fed is too late in cutting the interest rates, it is possible to see a mild recession in the second half of the year or in 2026.
  4. Individual stock risks: The second portfolio holds 10 individual stocks, amounting to 70% of the portfolio capital. Individual stocks have higher risks compared to a portfolio of funds, which would be much more diversified. The business prospects of an individual company can change due to many factors, such as new competition, product obsolescence, financial stress, or a change in management.
  5. Peak market risk: The broader market is near its all-time highs once again. Many of the funds and securities are relatively overvalued. Buying anything lump sum can involve more risk than buying in a phased approach.

Conclusion:

We have presented two very different approaches to investing, but aim to achieve very similar goals. The first strategy is more of a hands-off approach, while the second one is a DIY (do it yourself) type of approach. The second approach would only suit very active investors, whereas the first approach is much more appropriate for most retirees.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Weekly Indicators: USD And Consumer Spending Fade, While Oil Spikes Mean Stagflation?
data.description:
Purpose

I look at the high frequency weekly indicators because while they can be very noisy, they provide a good nowcast of the economy, and will telegraph the maintenance or change in the economy well before monthly or quarterly data is available. They are also an excellent way to "mark your beliefs to the market." In general, I go in order of long leading indicators, then short leading indicators, then coincident indicators.

A Note on Methodology

Data is presented in a "just the facts, ma'am" format with a minimum of commentary so that bias is minimized.

Where relevant, I include 12-month highs and lows in the data in parentheses to the right. All data taken from St. Louis FRED unless otherwise linked.

A few items (e.g., Financial Conditions indexes, regional Fed indexes, stock prices, the yield curve) have their own metrics based on long-term studies of their behavior.

Where data is seasonally adjusted, generally it is scored positively if it is within the top 1/3 of that range, negative in the bottom 1/3, and neutral in between. Where it is not seasonally adjusted, and there are seasonal issues, waiting for the YoY change to change sign will lag the turning point. Thus, I make use of a convention: data is scored neutral if it is less than 1/2 as positive/negative as at its 12-month extreme.

With long leading indicators, which by definition turn at least 12 months before a turning point in the economy as a whole, there is an additional rule: data is automatically negative if, during an expansion, it has not made a new peak in the past year, with the sole exception that it is scored neutral if it is moving in the right direction and is close to making a new high.

For all series where a graph is available, I have provided a link to where the relevant graph can be found.

No recession has happened without a YoY real M1 negative, or YoY real M2 below +2.5%. Real M2 fell below that threshold in March 2022. Real M1 also turned negative as of May 2022. The Miller Score is designed to look 52 weeks ahead for whether or not a recession is possible. Any score over 500 means no recession. This number fell below that threshold at the beginning of August 2021, so not only is it negative, but we are now years into the “recession eligible” time period. This indicator is heavily influenced by both the yield curve and the still very low unemployment rate vs. gas prices(expecting a reversal) which explains why it remains negative. My colleague who compiles this score began disaggregating the two components a number of weeks ago.

The St. Louis Financial Stress index is one where a negative score is a positive for the economy, and during its limited existence, has risen above zero before a recession by less than one year. It remained very positive until briefly in April. It then recovered and is near its best post-pandemic readings.

For over a year, IM has stated that their system does not forecast a recession.

The weekly result is derived simply by subtracting YoY inflation as measured by Truflation from the YoY change in nominal consumer spending as measured by Redbook. While it will be somewhat noisy, it should anticipate changes in the monthly measures ahead of time. It backed off from the 12-month high it set in February, and then gradually rebounded, and made another 12 month high several months ago. After mainly weeks in the upper part of its 12-month range, it was tepid at the beginning of the year, before improving in the past two months, even making a new 12-month high before backing off. With nominal spending relatively low and inflation holding generally steady, this indicator has weakened, although it is still positive.

Rail was mixed for many weeks before turning positive in March, presumably reflecting businesses attempting to get inventory before the new tariffs kick in. Port sources have shown a significant decline in docking at West Coast ports since early April. Intermodal rail loads have steadily decelerated since then, and this week turned negative.

Harpex was as high as nearly 4500 almost 2 years ago, but declined as low as 810 in December of last year before rising almost relentlessly since, setting repeated 12 month highs in the past three months. The usual interpretation for this is that demand is straining against supply, showing a strong global economy, and thus very positive.

Similarly, the BDI was in a generally declining trend throughout 2022 before bottoming at a three-year low of 530 in February 2023. The overall trend has been higher since then, rising to a high of 3346 last December, probably reflecting the Houthi attacks on shipping in the Red Sea. This year its low was 1309 in January, and its recent high was 2419 last March. It declined to the bottom 1/3rd of its one-year range during the rest of the year, but rebounded in the past eleven weeks back into its middle range, and has remained in that range.

I am wary of reading too much into price indexes like this, since they are heavily influenced by supply (as in, a huge overbuilding of ships in the last decade) as well as demand.

For the second week in a row, there was little significant change this week. But underneath, there were some pimportant changes.

The long leading indicators remain neutral. Monetary and financial positives are balancing housing and yield spreads. Of particular interest, estimated corporate profits are declining for the second quarter in a row.

The short leading indicators remained neutral for the fifth week in a row, as commodity and stock price improvements were offset by lower gas usage. But the US$ has continued to decline. If it declines by more than -10% YoY, I would begin to be concerned about a disorderly rout. Meanwhile, the direction of commodity prices is very different depending on whether or not oil is included. On Friday, oil prices spiked, as did total commodities, but commodities ex-oil were very weak.

The coincident indicators remain very positive, although tariff increases continue to hit the supply chain, and while consumer spending has faded somewhat, it is still up more than inflation YoY.

It is in volatile times like these that high-frequency indicators that are updated every week will first show the emerging trend. At this point, they are suggesting that the “stagflation” read is the most correct one.
